title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-3
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in California. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can offer supply at 6.85 per unit, which is in line with recent pricing and gives us room to commit dependable volume. If you have a target quantity or contract term in mind, I may be able to work with you on structure while keeping the pricing commercially workable. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor near the observed market reference of 6.50. The offer is set somewhat above market to preserve negotiating room while signaling willingness to discuss volume and term concessions.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.50.

steps.message: I appreciate the opening offer and the emphasis on dependable volume. That said, 6.85 is above where this program can work on our side at launch, so to get the conversation moving I can put forward 5.35 per unit as an initial counter. If there is flexibility tied to volume commitments or a longer contract term, we can explore structure from there, but we would need a materially sharper level than your opener. The supplier opened high but the latest market check is strong, so a meaningful counter is reasonable without walking away. This keeps the negotiation alive, signals budget discipline, and leaves room to adjust based on volume or term concessions in later rounds.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 5.35, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.63.

The offer is set somewhat above market to preserve negotiating room while signaling willingness to discuss volume and term concessions.

Observed market reference price: 7.60.

steps.message: Thanks for putting a concrete number on the table. At 5.35, we would be giving up too much relative to current market conditions, especially with pricing still holding firm, but I do want to keep this moving. I can come down to 6.35 per unit if we are talking about dependable volume and a clean launch commitment, and if you can improve the structure further on term or forecast visibility, I may have some room to work with you. The buyer made a low counter, but the latest market reference is strong enough to justify holding well above their number while still showing movement from our opening position.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement from your opening point, but 6.35 is still too far above where this works for us on a launch order. We do see the market holding up, so I’m not asking you to meet the prior 5.35 exactly; I can improve to 5.60 per unit if we pair that with dependable volume planning and a clean purchasing process. If you can narrow the gap further from there, we should have a realistic path to closing this quickly. The supplier moved down but remains well above the buyer’s target range. A modest concession keeps the negotiation active, acknowledges the strong market reference, and tests whether the seller is serious about finding the overlap zone without giving up too much margin too early.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 5.60, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.63.

steps.message: Thanks for narrowing the gap. Given the market reference we are seeing, 5.60 is still lighter than we can justify for a launch order, but I do want to keep this moving and recognize your flexibility. I can come down to 6.00 per unit if we pair it with dependable volume planning and a straightforward purchasing process, which puts us much closer to a workable close without stripping out too much value on our side. The buyer improved to 5.60, so a measured concession keeps momentum. The observed market reference is strong at 11.15, which supports holding above the buyer's level while still signaling willingness to close.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 6.00 and I do want to keep this progressing toward a practical close. That said, we still need enough room to protect our downstream economics, so the best next step I can make is 5.80 per unit if we can keep the order process clean and volumes reasonably predictable. This narrows the gap again and reflects a real concession from our side to try to land this quickly. The supplier moved down modestly, and a small counter keeps momentum without conceding all remaining room at once. The latest market reference is 7.55, so the seller has support to stay firm, but 5.80 is a credible bridging offer from the buyer's side.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 5.80, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.63.

The latest market reference is strong, which supports holding above the buyer’s last offer. A small concession keeps momentum without giving up too much value late in the negotiation.

steps.message: I appreciate the move to 6.20, and I want to keep this moving toward a close. From my side, 5.95 per unit is a serious step up from 5.80 and reflects that I’m trying to bridge the gap while still protecting the economics of the program. If we can firm things up at 5.95 with a straightforward order and dependable execution, I’m prepared to work to finalize this quickly. The supplier moved down modestly from 6.20, and the market reference is strong, so a small concession is more credible than a large jump. This keeps the negotiation alive near the end, improves from the buyer’s last position, and tests whether the seller will trade a little more to close.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 5.95, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.63.

steps.message: I appreciate the serious move to 5.95, and I want to close this rather than drag it out. I can narrow the gap one last step to 6.05 per unit, which is a modest concession from 6.20 and keeps the deal aligned with current market strength and our supply economics. If you can confirm at 6.05 with a clean order and dependable execution, I’m prepared to finalize immediately. The buyer made a credible late-stage offer, but with the market reference still materially stronger, holding a small premium is reasonable. A final, limited concession tests for close without giving away unnecessary margin at the end.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer ended in rejection.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal rejected. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
